questions.question: Define Proposition and give two examples. Also state what does not qualify as a proposition.
keyPoints: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either TRUE or FALSE, but not both
Example 1: 'India is a country' (TRUE)
Example 2: '2+2=5' (FALSE)
Statements that are NOT propositions: Questions, commands, opinions, or statements with variables (like 'x > 5')

questions.question: Write truth tables for AND (∧), OR (∨), NOT (¬), and IMPLIES (→) connectives.
keyPoints: AND (∧): T∧T=T, all others F
OR (∨): F∨F=F, all others T
NOT (¬): ¬T=F, ¬F=T
IMPLIES (→): Only F when T→F, all others T

questions.question: Explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with one example each.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens: [(p→q) ∧ p] ⇒ q - If p implies q, and p is true, then q is true
Example: If it rains (p), ground gets wet (q). It is raining. Therefore, ground is wet.
Modus Tollens: [(p→q) ∧ ¬q] ⇒ ¬p - If p implies q, and q is false, then p is false
Example: If it rains (p), ground gets wet (q). Ground is not wet. Therefore, it is not raining.

questions.question: Differentiate between Tautology, Contradiction, and Contingency with one example each.
keyPoints: Tautology: Always TRUE - example: p ∨ ¬p (Law of Excluded Middle)
Contradiction: Always FALSE - example: p ∧ ¬p (cannot be both true and false)
Contingency: TRUE or FALSE depending on values - example: p → q (depends on p and q values)

questions.question: Explain Universal and Existential quantifiers with examples. Also write their negations.
keyPoints: Universal Quantifier (∀): 'For all' - ∀x P(x) means property holds for every element
Example: ∀x (x + 0 = x) - For all x, x+0 equals x
Existential Quantifier (∃): 'There exists' - ∃x P(x) means at least one element satisfies property
Example: ∃x (x² = 9) - There exists x such that x² = 9 (x=3 or x=-3)
Negation: ¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x); ¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x)

questions.question: What is Logical Equivalence? Prove that ¬(p ∧ q) ≡ (¬p) ∨ (¬q) using truth table.
keyPoints: Logical Equivalence: Two propositions have identical truth values for all possible truth assignments
Prove ¬(p ∧ q) ≡ (¬p) ∨ (¬q) by showing both have same truth values for all 4 combinations of p and q: (T,T): ¬F=F, F∨F=F; (T,F): ¬F=T, F∨T=T; (F,T): ¬F=T, T∨T=T; (F,F): ¬F=T, T∨T=T

questions.question: What are the bound and free variables in the following predicate formula: ∀x (P(x) → ∃y Q(x,y) ∧ R(z))
keyPoints: Bound variables: x is bound by ∀x, y is bound by ∃y
Free variable: z is free (not within any quantifier scope)
Explanation: Variables within the scope of a quantifier are bound; those outside retain their independent meaning

questions.question: What is an argument in logic? When is an argument valid? Determine if the following argument is valid: Premise 1: If it rains, the street gets wet. Premise 2: The street is not wet. Conclusion: Therefore, it did not rain.
keyPoints: An argument is a set of propositions (premises) followed by a conclusion
An argument is valid if the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises - if all premises are true, conclusion MUST be true
Modus Tollens form: p→q, ¬q ∴ ¬p
p = it rains, q = street gets wet
Premise 1: p→q (If it rains, street gets wet) - TRUE
Premise 2: ¬q (Street is not wet) - TRUE
Conclusion: ¬p (It did not rain) - By Modus Tollens, this follows necessarily
Therefore, the argument is VALID

questions.question: Explain Predicate Logic with its components. Differentiate between atomic formulas and well-formed formulas with examples.
keyPoints: Predicate Logic extends Propositional Logic by dealing with predicates and quantifiers
Components: Predicates (properties/relations), Terms (constants, variables, functions), Quantifiers (∀, ∃)
Atomic Formula: Basic unit - predicate applied to terms. Examples: P(x), Likes(John, Mary), GreaterThan(5, x)
Well-Formed Formula (WFF): Valid formula built using: atomic formulas + connectives + quantifiers + parentheses
Examples of WFFs: ¬P(x), P(x) ∧ Q(x), ∀x (P(x) → ∃y R(x,y))
Not WFF: →P(x), ∀x P (missing predicate argument)

questions.question: Construct the truth table for p ↔ q (biconditional). Prove that p ↔ q is logically equivalent to (p → q) ∧ (q → p). Also state two real-world examples of biconditional.
keyPoints: Truth table for p↔q: T↔T=T, T↔F=F, F↔T=F, F↔F=T (TRUE when both have SAME truth values)
Equivalence proof: Show (p→q)∧(q→p) has identical truth values: Both are T when p=T,q=T and p=F,q=F; F otherwise
Real-world examples: 1) 'You can vote if and only if you are 18 or older' - both directions; 2) 'A triangle is equilateral if and only if all its angles are 60°'

questions.question: Explain the following with examples: (i) Hypothetical Syllogism (ii) Disjunctive Syllogism (iii) Constructive Dilemma. Also determine if the argument 'If I study, I will pass. If I pass, I will get a job. Therefore, if I study, I will get a job' is valid.
keyPoints: (i) Hypothetical Syllogism: P→Q, Q→R ∴ P→R (chain rule)
Example: If A, then B. If B, then C. Therefore, if A then C.
(ii) Disjunctive Syllogism: P∨Q, ¬P ∴ Q
Example: Either it rains or it shines. It does not rain. Therefore, it shines.
(iii) Constructive Dilemma: (P→Q) ∧ (R→S), P∨R ∴ Q∨S

Given argument: P=study, Q=pass, R=pass, S=get job
Premise 1: P→Q (study→pass) TRUE
Premise 2: Q→S (pass→job) TRUE
Conclusion: P→S (study→job) - By Hypothetical Syllogism, VALID
